    Hourglass Magnetic Field Morphology

    Hourglass B-Field

    Type:Observational signature / morphology
    Indicates:Magnetically regulated gravitational collapse
    Observed in:OMC-1, DR21, G351.77-0.53, NGC 1333 IRAS 4A
    Related: B-fields in star formation
    Gravitational collapse
    Dust polarimetry

    The hourglass magnetic field morphology is a distinctive pinched, waist-like pattern in magnetic field lines observed toward collapsing cloud cores and clumps. It arises when gravitational contraction drags initially ordered (often perpendicular-to-filament) field lines inward, concentrating them toward the densest region. The hourglass shape is one of the strongest observational signatures that magnetic fields are dynamically important and that collapse is magnetically regulated[1][2].

    Physical Origin

    Gravitational Pinching

    In the classic picture, a cloud with initially uniform B-field perpendicular to a filament undergoes gravitational contraction. Because field lines are frozen into the (partially) ionized gas, the collapsing material drags field lines inward, creating a pinched hourglass geometry centered on the densest clump[1]. MHD simulations by Nakamura & Li (2008) show this arises naturally when turbulence cannot support the cloud against settling along field lines, and ambipolar diffusion allows dense cores to become self-gravitating[3].

    Converging Sub-filaments

    An alternative mechanism produces hourglass-like morphology through converging sub-filaments aligned with the B-field. As material flows along multiple sub-filaments toward a central hub, the field lines — parallel to the low-density striations but perpendicular to the dense spine — naturally produce a pinched pattern[4][5]. Both mechanisms may act simultaneously.

    Observational Examples

    • G351.77-0.53 — SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm polarimetry reveals a clear hourglass toward clump c1, with B_POS reaching ~0.8 mG. The cloud is magnetically transcritical (λ ~ 0.9) on large scales, transitioning to supercritical near c1[6].
    • OMC-1 (Orion A) — JCMT BISTRO and SOFIA/HAWC+ show hourglass morphology with magnetic energy dominating the energy budget[7][8]
    • DR21 — JCMT BISTRO-2 shows perpendicular B-fields at pc-scales with hourglass toward the southern massive core[9]
    • Serpens South — B-field lines follow inflowing filaments to the main hub[5]

    Diagnostic Power

    The hourglass morphology directly constrains the mass-to-flux ratio and collapse geometry. Combined with the DCF method, it allows estimation of field strength at different scales. The transition from transcritical (cloud envelope) to supercritical (core) mass-to-flux ratios is a prediction of magnetically regulated collapse models that can be tested in hourglass sources[6].

    However, distinguishing gravitational pinching from sub-filament convergence requires high-resolution polarimetry combined with kinematic data to identify infall signatures[6].

    See Also

    • Jadhav et al. (2025) — hourglass in G351.77-0.53
    • Magnetic fields in star formation
    • Gravitational collapse
    • Dust polarimetry
    • Mass-to-flux ratio
    • DCF method
    • Filamentary star formation

    References
    1. Mouschovias, T. Ch. (1976). "Magnetic support of interstellar clouds." ApJ, 207, 141. DOI:10.1086/154835
    2. Chandrasekhar, S. & Fermi, E. (1953). "Magnetic Fields in Spiral Arms." ApJ, 118, 113. DOI:10.1086/145731
    3. Nakamura, F. & Li, Z.-Y. (2008). "Magnetically regulated star formation." ApJ, 687, 354. DOI:10.1086/591641
    4. Gómez, G. C., et al. (2018). "B-field bending by filament self-gravity." MNRAS, 480, 2939. DOI:10.1093/mnras/sty2018
    5. Pillai, T. G. S., et al. (2020). "Magnetized gas flows in Serpens South." Nature Astron., 4, 1195. DOI:10.1038/s41550-020-1172-6
    6. Jadhav, O. R., et al. (2025). "Hourglass B-field toward IRDC G351.77-0.53." arXiv:2603.16425
    7. Pattle, K., et al. (2017). "JCMT BISTRO: OMC-1 B-field." ApJ, 846, 122. DOI:10.3847/1538-4357/aa80e5
    8. Chuss, D. T., et al. (2019). "SOFIA/HAWC+ OMC-1 polarimetry." ApJ, 872, 187. DOI:10.3847/1538-4357/aafd37
    9. Ching, T.-C., et al. (2022). "DR21 JCMT BISTRO-2." ApJ, 941, 122. DOI:10.3847/1538-4357/ac9dfb
